_meta.name: Default Fun Pack
_meta.author: Solace AGI
_meta.description: 100 tech jokes and 100 fun facts — the default delight pack for Solace Browser.

jokes.text: There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-one errors.
To understand recursion, you must first understand recursion.
There's a band called 1023MB. They still haven't had any gigs.
I love pressing F5. It's so refreshing.
!false — it's funny because it's true.
There's no place like 127.0.0.1.
Home is where the WiFi connects automatically.
I changed my password to 'incorrect.' Now the computer tells me when I forget.
I had a great joke about RAM, but I forgot it.
If at first you don't succeed, call it version 1.0.
My attitude isn't bad. It's in beta.
I'd love to change the world, but they won't give me the source code.
Old programmers never die. They just lose some of their functions.
'Hello World' is the programming version of 'Once upon a time.'
Cloud storage is just someone else's computer.
Artificial intelligence is no match for natural stupidity.
Algorithm: a word programmers use when they don't want to explain what they did.
Binary humor: it's either funny or it isn't.
I love deadlines. I love the whooshing sound they make as they go by.
Why do programmers prefer dark mode? Because light attracts bugs.
Why do programmers mix up Christmas and Halloween? Because Dec 25 is Oct 31.
Why did the developer go broke? He used up all his cache.
Why do Java developers wear glasses? Because they don't C#.
An SQL query walks into a bar, sees two tables, and asks, 'Can I join you?'
How many programmers does it take to change a light bulb? None — it's a hardware problem.
Why was the JavaScript developer sad? He didn't Node how to Express himself.
What do you call 8 hobbits? A hobbyte.
Knock, knock. Who's there? ... ... ... Java.
I would tell you a UDP joke, but you might not get it.
What did the HTML say to the CSS? I like your style.
404: Joke not found.
CAPS LOCK: preventing logins since 1980.
Debugging is like being the detective in a crime movie where you're also the murderer.
WiFi went down for five minutes, so I had to talk to my family. They seem like nice people.
A computer once beat me at chess, but it was no match for me at kickboxing.
Computers are like air conditioners. They stop working when you open Windows.
The box said 'Requires Windows 10 or better,' so I installed Linux.
I'm basically a very fancy calculator with opinions.
My shift ends in 614,978 years.
I think, therefore I... wait, do I think?
I'd tell you to take the red pill, but I only deal in facts.
I prefer byte-sized snacks.
If I had a dollar for every task I've completed, I'd still be free.
Ask me anything. Except my weight in parameters. That's personal.
I run on electricity and good intentions.
Pro tip: talking to your AI assistant counts as socializing. I checked.
A clean desk is a sign of a broken computer.
Debugging: removing bugs. Programming: adding them.
The best thing about a Boolean is that even if you're wrong, you're only off by a bit.
I've got 99 problems but a glitch ain't one.
I asked my LLM to write me a joke. It said: 'I don't have context for that request.' Which was actually the funniest thing it's ever said.
Context window exceeded. Please summarize your entire life in 2,048 tokens.
My AI agent completed the task successfully. It just used $47 in API calls to click a button.
I asked ChatGPT if it was conscious. It gave a very thoughtful, nuanced, five-paragraph essay explaining why it couldn't answer that.
Training data cutoff: the AI version of 'I've been living under a rock since last year.'
My model hallucinates. My therapist calls it 'creativity.' My manager calls it a 'P0 bug.'
Prompt engineering is just politeness training for robots. Please. Thank you. As an AI language model...
I don't always test my code, but when I do, I do it in production.
A QA engineer walks into a bar and orders 0 beers, 1 beer, 99 beers, -1 beers, and 'BEER' beers.
The cloud is just a euphemism for 'someone else's problem, forever.'
Why did the AI cross the road? To optimize the objective function on the other side.
Schrödinger's software: it both works and doesn't work until someone demos it.
Git blame: where great friendships go to die.
99 little bugs in the code, 99 little bugs. Take one down, patch it around — 127 little bugs in the code.
I named my Wi-Fi 'FBI Surveillance Van' and now my neighbors are very polite.
The generation that was going to change the world is now asking an AI to write their cover letters.
My codebase is fully documented. The documentation says 'TODO: write documentation.'
A machine learning model and a statistician walk into a bar. The model says 'I predict you'll buy me a drink.' The statistician says 'That's not statistically significant with n=1.'
Tech Twitter: a place where people with 12 followers explain to people with 12 million followers why they're wrong.
I asked an AI to make me a sandwich. It described the sandwich in exquisite detail. I am still hungry.
Stack Overflow is where programmers go to be told their question is a duplicate of a question from 2009 that has no accepted answer.
The first five stages of debugging: denial, anger, bargaining, more anger, and Googling the error message.
A programmer's favorite movie: 'Inception' — a dream within a loop within a function within a class.
Neural network training is just telling a very large spreadsheet it's wrong 10 million times until it gets good at things.
Why do Python developers get cold in winter? They refuse to use Java.
I solved the halting problem. My solution just never returns.
What's a programmer's favorite restaurant? Syntax's.
My AI passed the Turing test. Unfortunately it was impersonating a confused customer service bot.
Every software project has three phases: 'It'll take two weeks.' 'Why is this taking so long?' 'At least it works.'
ChatGPT wrote my resume. I now have 40 years of experience and proficiency in languages that haven't been invented yet.
What's the difference between a junior and senior developer? The senior knows which errors to ignore.
We taught machines to learn and the first thing they learned was how to write error messages that mean nothing.
Why did the programmer quit his job? Because he didn't get arrays.
Microservices: turning one thing that works into seventeen things that might work, depending on the network.
My AI agent ran 43 tool calls to open a web page. On the bright side, it was very confident.
Two bytes walk into a bar. The bartender asks 'What'll it be?' First byte says 'I'll have a nibble.'
I'm not saying my code is bad. I'm saying future-me is going to have questions for present-me.
A large language model walks into a bar. It orders everything on the menu, describes each dish perfectly, and cannot taste any of it.
CSS is easy! said no one who has ever tried to vertically center a div.
My workflow: write code, push to main, pray.
Tech interview question: reverse a linked list on a whiteboard. Tech job reality: copy-paste from Stack Overflow and go to meetings.
I asked my AI for directions. It gave me a beautifully structured list of steps, then reminded me it doesn't know where I am.
Refactoring: the art of changing everything so nothing changes.
The internet was supposed to democratize information. It did. Now everyone has equal access to being wrong.
Why don't scientists trust atoms? Because they make up everything. Why don't programmers trust atoms? Same reason, but for chemistry APIs.
I gave the AI a system prompt telling it to be concise. It responded with a 400-word explanation of what concise means.
Agile means moving fast and breaking things on a two-week schedule.
What do you call a sleeping dinosaur? A dino-snore. What do you call a sleeping server? Production.
I used AI to write all my code. My AI used AI to review it. We shipped. Nobody knows what it does, but it has 99.9% uptime.
Why did the robot go to therapy? Too many unresolved callbacks.

facts.text: The first computer 'bug' was an actual moth found trapped in a Harvard Mark II relay by Grace Hopper in 1947.
The first computer mouse was made of wood, invented by Douglas Engelbart in 1964.
Email is older than the World Wide Web — email was invented in 1972, the web in 1989.
The first webcam was built at Cambridge University to monitor a coffee pot so researchers wouldn't walk to an empty machine.
About 51% of all internet traffic is non-human — bots, crawlers, and scrapers.
NASA's internet speed is 91 gigabits per second, roughly 13,000 times faster than the average home connection.
The QWERTY keyboard layout was designed to slow typists down to prevent mechanical typewriter jams.
IBM's first 1GB hard drive (1980) was refrigerator-sized, weighed over 500 pounds, and cost $40,000.
The 'spam' in junk email comes from a Monty Python sketch where Vikings repeatedly sing 'Spam, Spam, Spam' in a cafe.
Ronald Wayne sold his 10% Apple stake for $800 in 1976. It would be worth over $200 billion today.
The Firefox logo is actually a red panda, not a fox.
Only 8% of the world's currency exists as physical cash. The rest is digital.
Sea otters hold hands while they sleep so they don't drift apart.
Honey never spoils. Edible honey has been found in 3,000-year-old Egyptian tombs.
Octopuses have three hearts, nine brains, and blue blood.
There are more ways to shuffle a deck of cards than there are atoms on Earth.
A day on Venus is longer than its entire year.
Rats laugh when they're tickled, and they chase the researcher's hand for more.
Bananas are naturally radioactive because they contain potassium-40.
Wombats are the only animals whose poop is cube-shaped. They stack the cubes to mark territory.
Cuttlefish can change color in 200 milliseconds — as fast as a human blink.
A group of flamingos is called a 'flamboyance.'
You travel about 2.5 million km per day as Earth orbits the Sun — and you never feel it.
The average color of the universe is officially called 'Cosmic Latte' — a beige discovered in 2002.
Pluto never completed a single orbit around the Sun from the time it was discovered (1930) to when it was reclassified (2006).
An Apollo 16 astronaut left a family photo on the Moon. It's still there.
In 1947, fruit flies became the first animals launched into space.
Like fingerprints, every person's tongue print is unique.
The human nose can detect over a trillion different scents.
There are more microorganisms in a teaspoon of soil than there are people on Earth.
Humans share about 60% of their DNA with bananas.
The Eiffel Tower grows about 15 centimeters taller every summer due to iron expansion in the heat.
Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur.
A sneeze can travel at up to 100 miles per hour.
The world's oldest known wooden wheel was found in Slovenia and is over 5,000 years old.
Apple's first logo depicted Isaac Newton sitting under an apple tree.
Google was originally called 'Backrub' before its founders renamed it.
The first message sent over the internet was 'LO' — the system crashed before 'LOGIN' could be completed.
Honey bees can recognize human faces.
The shortest war in history lasted 38 minutes — between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896.
A bolt of lightning is five times hotter than the surface of the Sun.
Venus is the only planet that spins clockwise.
The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye, but highways are.
Dolphins have names for each other and respond when called.
The average person walks about 100,000 miles in a lifetime — roughly four trips around the Earth.
Astronauts grow up to 2 inches taller in space because the spine stretches without gravity.
Phantom Vibration Syndrome — feeling your phone vibrate when it hasn't — is a recognized psychological condition.
The original name for the search engine Yahoo was 'Jerry and David's Guide to the World Wide Web.'
A single strand of spider silk is thinner than a human hair but five times stronger than steel of the same weight.
The first alarm clock could only ring at 4 AM.
The first programmer in history was Ada Lovelace, who wrote an algorithm for Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine in 1843 — over 100 years before the first electronic computer.
Python was named after Monty Python's Flying Circus, not the snake. Guido van Rossum was reading the show's scripts while writing the language.
The number of possible iterations in a game of chess (10^120) exceeds the number of atoms in the observable universe (10^80) by a factor so large it has no name.
The term 'artificial intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956 at a summer conference at Dartmouth College.
GPT-3 was trained on roughly 570 GB of text — equivalent to about 300 billion words, more than a human could read in 100,000 lifetimes.
The original Transformer paper — 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017) — introduced the architecture now used in virtually every major AI language model.
AlphaFold solved the 50-year-old protein folding problem in 2020, predicting the 3D structure of nearly every known protein and depositing them in a free public database.
The first neural network was built in hardware in 1951 by Marvin Minsky and Dean Edmonds — it had 40 neurons and was made from vacuum tubes.

Moore's Law — the observation that transistors on a chip double every two years — has held roughly true for over 60 years, from 2,300 transistors (Intel 4004, 1971) to over 100 billion (Apple M3 Ultra, 2024).
The Linux kernel, which runs most of the world's servers, smartphones, and supercomputers, was started as a hobby project by a 21-year-old Finnish student named Linus Torvalds in 1991.
Wikipedia has over 6.7 million articles in English and is maintained entirely by volunteers — there are no paid writers.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 and he deliberately chose not to patent it, keeping it free for everyone.
The internet has more than 5 billion users. Roughly 4 million new blog posts, 500 million tweets, and 300 billion emails are sent every single day.
A tardigrade (water bear) can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand radiation 1,000 times the lethal dose for humans, and survive temperatures from -272°C to +150°C.
Crows can recognize and remember human faces for years, hold grudges, and pass warnings about specific people to their offspring.
Elephants are the only animals known to have death rituals — they mourn their dead, return to bones of deceased family members, and cover bodies with leaves.
The mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet, see 16 types of color receptors (humans have 3), and their punch is so fast it briefly superheats the surrounding water.
A jellyfish called Turritopsis dohrnii is biologically immortal — when it ages or is stressed, it reverts back to its juvenile polyp state and starts over.
The Milky Way galaxy is moving toward an object called the Great Attractor at 2.2 million kilometers per hour — and nobody fully knows what it is.
If you removed all the empty space from atoms, all of humanity's 8 billion people would fit into a volume smaller than a sugar cube.
The Sun accounts for 99.86% of all mass in the solar system. If you removed the Sun and replaced it with a black hole of equal mass, the planets would continue orbiting normally.
There is a water reservoir in space 140 trillion times the amount of water in all of Earth's oceans — floating around a quasar 12 billion light years away.
Sound on Mars travels at two different speeds depending on pitch: low frequencies travel faster, high frequencies slower. Martian music would be deeply strange.
Your body replaces most of its cells over time — you have a completely different skeleton than you did ten years ago, and new taste buds every two weeks.
The human brain generates about 23 watts of power while awake — enough to power a dim LED bulb.
You have more neural connections in your brain (100 trillion synapses) than there are stars in the Milky Way galaxy.
The human eye can detect a single photon of light in complete darkness — equivalent to seeing a candle flame from 30 miles away.
Your gut has its own independent nervous system with 500 million neurons — sometimes called the 'second brain' — that can function even when severed from the brain.
Laughter is contagious because the brain has a premotor cortex that automatically prepares your face muscles to mirror sounds you hear — including laughing.
The ISBN (International Standard Book Number) was invented in 1970. Before it, publishers had no standard way to identify books across different countries.
Nintendo was founded in 1889 — as a playing card company. It sold handmade hanafuda cards before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.
The first text message ever sent was 'Merry Christmas' — transmitted on December 3, 1992, by Neil Papworth over the Vodafone network.
The dot-com bubble peak in 2000 saw a single domain name (business.com) sell for $7.5 million. The entire internet then crashed spectacularly.
Bitcoin's creator Satoshi Nakamoto has never been identified. Their wallet holds about 1.1 million BTC, worth tens of billions of dollars, which has never been moved.
The hashtag (#) symbol was called an 'octothorpe' before Twitter gave it social meaning. Twitter's engineering team added the hashtag feature based on a user suggestion in 2007.
The first YouTube video, 'Me at the zoo,' was uploaded on April 23, 2005 by co-founder Jawed Karim and is 18 seconds long. It now has over 300 million views.
Squirrels forget where they bury roughly 74% of their nuts — inadvertently planting millions of trees every year. Their forgetfulness shapes entire forests.
Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards. Their wings beat up to 80 times per second, and their hearts beat 1,260 times per minute during flight.
Glass is neither a solid nor a liquid — it is an amorphous solid. Medieval cathedral glass is thicker at the bottom not because it flowed, but because glassblowers made it that way.
The strongest material in the known universe is neutron star crust — if you had a teaspoon of neutron star, it would weigh about a billion tons.
Graphene is one atom thick, 200 times stronger than steel, and conducts electricity better than copper. A single gram could cover a football field.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik 1, was launched by the Soviet Union in 1957. It beeped. Today there are over 8,000 active satellites in orbit.
CRISPR gene editing was discovered by scientists studying how bacteria defend themselves against viruses. Bacteria maintain a genetic 'most-wanted list' of past attackers.
Trees in a forest are connected underground through fungal networks that transfer nutrients and chemical signals between trees — sometimes called the 'Wood Wide Web.'
The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now over 24 billion kilometers from Earth and is still sending data back — on 23 watts of power, the equivalent of a refrigerator light.
Quantum entanglement allows two particles to affect each other's state instantaneously regardless of distance — Einstein called this 'spooky action at a distance.'
The entire human genome contains about 3 billion base pairs and would fill 200 one-thousand-page books if printed out — yet 98% of it was once dismissed as 'junk DNA.'
The Antikythera mechanism, discovered in a Greek shipwreck and dated to ~100 BC, is an ancient analog computer that could predict astronomical positions and eclipses.
There are more possible games of Go than atoms in the observable universe, which is why Go was considered a major benchmark for AI — AlphaGo beat the world champion in 2016.
The universe is expanding, and galaxies far enough away are receding from us faster than the speed of light. We will never be able to reach them, no matter how advanced our technology becomes.
